title: SQL Injection in Login Endpoint
description: User-supplied email is interpolated directly into a raw Sequelize SQL query: `SELECT * FROM Users WHERE email = '${req.body.email}'...`. An attacker can inject SQL syntax to bypass authentication entirely (e.g., `' OR 1=1--`). No parameterized query or prepared statement is used.

title: SQL Injection in Product Search
description: The search criteria parameter is concatenated directly into a raw SQL query: `SELECT * FROM Products WHERE name LIKE '%${criteria}%'...`. An attacker can inject UNION-based payloads to exfiltrate all database content including user credentials. No sanitization or parameterization is applied.

title: Remote Code Execution via eval() on Username
description: The application calls `eval(code)` where `code` is derived from the user-controlled username field matching the pattern `#{...}`. Any attacker who can set their username to `#{require('child_process').execSync('id')}` can execute arbitrary OS commands on the server. This is a direct RCE vulnerability.

title: Server-Side Template Injection via Pug
description: User-controlled data is passed into `pug.compile(template)` without sanitization, enabling server-side template injection. An attacker with control over the template string can execute arbitrary JavaScript in the Node.js context, leading to full server compromise.

title: NoSQL Injection via $where in Order Tracking
description: A MongoDB query uses the `$where` operator with unsanitized user input: `db.ordersCollection.find({ $where: \`this.orderId === '${id}'\` })`. The `$where` clause executes JavaScript on the MongoDB server, allowing injection of arbitrary JS to bypass query logic or cause denial of service.

title: NoSQL Injection via $where in Product Reviews
description: Product reviews are fetched with `db.reviewsCollection.find({ $where: 'this.product == ' + id })` where `id` comes directly from the URL parameter. An attacker can inject JavaScript to bypass the filter or exfiltrate other documents from the collection.

title: Hardcoded RSA Private Key Used to Sign JWTs
description: The RSA private key for signing JWT authentication tokens is hardcoded as a string literal in source code. Since this key is committed to version control, any attacker with repository access can forge valid JWTs for any user account, achieving a complete authentication bypass.

title: Passwords Hashed with MD5 (No Salt)
description: The `hash()` function uses `crypto.createHash('md5')` to hash passwords before storage and comparison. MD5 is cryptographically broken, runs at billions of hashes per second on modern GPUs, and has no salt, making stored hashes trivially crackable via rainbow tables or brute force.

title: XXE via External Entity Processing in XML Upload
description: XML files are parsed with `libxml.parseXml(data, { noent: true })`, which enables external entity resolution. An attacker can upload an XML file with an `<!ENTITY>` referencing a local file (e.g., `/etc/passwd`) or an internal URL to perform XXE file disclosure or server-side request forgery.

title: Unsafe YAML Deserialization in File Upload
description: Uploaded YAML files are parsed with `yaml.load(data)` (unsafe mode) inside a VM sandbox. The `yaml.load()` function in older js-yaml versions can deserialize arbitrary JavaScript objects or execute code via specially crafted YAML documents, bypassing the intended sandboxing.

title: Open Redirect via Substring-Based Allowlist Bypass
description: The redirect validation function uses `url.includes(allowedUrl)` to check if a redirect target is whitelisted. An attacker can craft a URL containing a whitelisted domain as a substring (e.g., `https://attacker.com?x=https://github.com/juice-shop`) to bypass the check and redirect victims to arbitrary sites for phishing.

title: Path Traversal in Data Erasure Layout Parameter
description: The `req.body.layout` value is passed to `path.resolve()` and then used in `res.render()` without adequate path restriction. The denylist checks for substrings like 'ftp' and 'encryptionkeys' but can be bypassed with case variations or alternative path encodings, allowing an attacker to render arbitrary local template files.

title: Insecure Direct Object Reference on Basket Access
description: The basket endpoint accesses basket data using a user-supplied basket ID from the URL without verifying that the authenticated user owns that basket. An attacker can enumerate basket IDs to view or modify other users' shopping carts and order data.

title: IDOR on Payment Methods via User-Supplied UserId
description: Payment card lookup uses `UserId` from `req.body` rather than extracting it exclusively from the authenticated JWT. If the middleware appending the user ID can be bypassed or if the body value takes precedence, an attacker can supply an arbitrary `UserId` to access another user's saved payment methods.

title: Critically Outdated JWT Libraries
description: The application depends on `jsonwebtoken@0.4.0` (2014) and `express-jwt@0.1.3` (2013), versions with known vulnerabilities including algorithm confusion attacks (accepting `alg: none`) and signature bypass flaws. Current versions are 9.x and 8.x respectively. These libraries handle authentication tokens for all users.

title: XSS Protection Header Explicitly Disabled
description: The Helmet middleware configuration explicitly disables the `X-XSS-Protection` header and does not configure a strict Content Security Policy. This removes a browser-level defense layer and makes reflected XSS attacks more impactful across all application pages.

title: Rate Limiting on Password Reset Bypassable via Header Casing
description: The rate limiting implementation for password reset uses case-sensitive header matching, which may allow an attacker to bypass limits by varying the case of header names. This enables unlimited password reset attempts, facilitating account takeover via token enumeration.

title: Insufficient Input Length Check Does Not Prevent SQL Injection
description: A 200-character length truncation is applied to the search criteria before it is concatenated into the SQL query. This length check is wholly inadequate as SQL injection payloads can be shorter than 200 characters. It creates a false sense of security while leaving the injection vector open.
